methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $640M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $6.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Redwood Materials
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series D), post-money $6B+

data.narrative:
Solugen, a Series D climate and energy technology company, has raised $640M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a peer set of four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage private companies: Redwood Materials (Series D, $6.0B), KoBold Metals (Series C, $2.0B), Electric Hydrogen (Series C, $1.0B), and Amogy (Series C, $700M). This peer universe sits within a broader landscape of nine private frontier-tech companies and one public comparable (Linacoln Electric, LNZA) in the Climate & Energy sector.

Two valuation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, using total raised as a proxy baseline, applies a typical 1.5x step-up multiplier to advance from Series D to the next financing stage, yielding $960M. Peer-median analysis anchors to the $1.5B median valuation across the four-company sample, with a range spanning $700M to $6.0B.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $816M to $1.7B, with a median of $1.2B. The wide dispersion in peer valuations—driven primarily by Redwood Materials' $6.0B valuation—introduces material uncertainty around the appropriate comparable multiple. Stage-progression and peer-median methods yield differing results, suggesting sensitivity to methodology selection and the representativeness of individual comps within an inherently heterogeneous frontier-tech sector.
